title: Stab-sewn zine
subtitle: Binding a small-run zine with a visible stab stitch through the cover and pages
excerpt: A stab-sewn binding sews through the stack of pages from the front cover to the back, leaving a visible stitch pattern on the spine edge. It is a practical binding for a zine of up to 40 pages: no signatures, no folding in sections, no adhesive. The stitch pattern can be as plain as a three-hole running stitch or as decorative as a Japanese four-hole stitch.
sourceNotes: Stab binding is an ancient technique documented in Japanese and European bookbinding literature. The application to contemporary self-published zines is a modern adaptation. No single pre-1928 English-language source covers the zine context specifically. Synthesised from contemporary craft documentation.

glossaryTerms.definition: A bookbinding method in which the needle and thread pierce through the full thickness of the page stack from front to back, rather than sewing through folded signatures.
A traditional Japanese stab-binding pattern using four sewing holes in an evenly spaced row, with a wrapping sequence that produces a visible grid of thread on the spine.

content.text: does not open fully flat: the pages are held tightly at the spine and the book opens at an angle. For a zine this is an acceptable trade-off. The advantage is speed and no adhesive: print, collate, clamp, pierce, sew, done.
Piercing through a thick stack of pages requires firm awl pressure. Hold the stack firmly in clamps while piercing so the pages do not shift. Keep fingers behind the awl tip.
Collate the printed pages in order and add a cover sheet (a single folded card or a heavier paper sheet) at front and back. Jog the stack square before clamping.
Thread: waxed linen thread holds the stab holes open as it passes through. Wax the thread by drawing it across a beeswax block before sewing.
Clamp the stack with bulldog clips on the fore-edge and head and tail if needed. This keeps pages from shifting during piercing.
Mark the sewing holes on the spine edge: for a four-hole stitch, place holes 8 mm from the spine edge, at 15 mm from head and tail, and at equal intervals in between.
Pierce each hole with the awl from the front cover through to the back. Work firmly and straight; angled holes misalign the thread line.
Thread the needle with waxed linen, three times the spine height. Enter through hole 1 from the front, leaving a 50 mm tail at the front.
Pass through all four holes in sequence: 1 to 2, 2 to 3, 3 to 4. Wrap around the head edge and re-enter hole 1. Wrap around the tail edge and re-enter hole 4. Run back through holes 3, 2, 1.
Tie off to the tail at hole 1. The finished stitch shows a
grid visible on the front and back covers.
